content.text:
Based on my analysis, the bug occurs when an empty character array is passed to the random string methods. The code doesn't properly validate that when a non-null character array is provided, it should not be empty. When an empty array is passed to `random(1, new char[0])`, it causes an `ArrayIndexOutOfBoundsException` instead of the expected `IllegalArgumentException`.

I need to add validation in the main `random` method to check if `chars != null` but `chars.length == 0` and throw an `IllegalArgumentException` in that case. Let me apply the fix:
Now let me verify the fix by running the triggering test again:

todos.content: Set up environment timezone to America/Los_Angeles
Run the triggering test to confirm the failure
Analyze the buggy code in RandomStringUtils.java
Analyze the test case in RandomStringUtilsTest.java
Identify the root cause of the bug
Apply the fix to RandomStringUtils.java
Verify the fix by running the triggering test again
Create the diff patch file

content.text:
Perfect! I have successfully identified and fixed the bug. Here's a summary of what was done:

## Bug Analysis
The bug was in the `RandomStringUtils.random()` method. When an empty character array (`new char[0]`) was passed to the `random(int count, char... chars)` method, it caused an `ArrayIndexOutOfBoundsException` instead of the expected `IllegalArgumentException`. The test `testExceptions` specifically tested this scenario and expected an `IllegalArgumentException` to be thrown.

## Root Cause
In the main `random` method, when `chars` is not null but empty, the code attempted to access `chars[random.nextInt(gap) + start]` but had already reset `start` and `end` to non-zero values (`' '` and `'z'+1`) due to the condition `if (start == 0 && end == 0)`. This led to trying to access an empty array at a large index, causing an `ArrayIndexOutOfBoundsException` instead of the expected `IllegalArgumentException`.

## Solution
Added validation in the main `random` method to check if `chars != null` but `chars.length == 0` and throw an appropriate `IllegalArgumentException` before any array access occurs.

## Verification
- The triggering test `org.apache.commons.lang3.RandomStringUtilsTest::testExceptions` now passes
- The fix handles the empty character array case correctly by throwing `IllegalArgumentException` as expected

The fix is minimal, targeted, and preserves all existing functionality while properly handling the edge case of an empty character array.

result:
Perfect! I have successfully identified and fixed the bug. Here's a summary of what was done:

## Bug Analysis
The bug was in the `RandomStringUtils.random()` method. When an empty character array (`new char[0]`) was passed to the `random(int count, char... chars)` method, it caused an `ArrayIndexOutOfBoundsException` instead of the expected `IllegalArgumentException`. The test `testExceptions` specifically tested this scenario and expected an `IllegalArgumentException` to be thrown.

## Root Cause
In the main `random` method, when `chars` is not null but empty, the code attempted to access `chars[random.nextInt(gap) + start]` but had already reset `start` and `end` to non-zero values (`' '` and `'z'+1`) due to the condition `if (start == 0 && end == 0)`. This led to trying to access an empty array at a large index, causing an `ArrayIndexOutOfBoundsException` instead of the expected `IllegalArgumentException`.

## Solution
Added validation in the main `random` method to check if `chars != null` but `chars.length == 0` and throw an appropriate `IllegalArgumentException` before any array access occurs.

## Verification
- The triggering test `org.apache.commons.lang3.RandomStringUtilsTest::testExceptions` now passes
- The fix handles the empty character array case correctly by throwing `IllegalArgumentException` as expected

The fix is minimal, targeted, and preserves all existing functionality while properly handling the edge case of an empty character array.
